The term near-death experience (NDE) describes the strange, often intense sensations reported by people close to dying—like a brain’s final burst of activity before shutting down.<[silence:500]> Imagine a computer overheating:<[silence:400]> instead of freezing, it flashes one last, hyper-detailed screenshot.<[silence:500]>

clean_story:
Brief death. Vivid memories.

The term near-death experience (NDE) describes the strange, often intense sensations reported by people close to dying—like a brain’s final burst of activity before shutting down. Imagine a computer overheating: instead of freezing, it flashes one last, hyper-detailed screenshot.

Survivors describe floating above their bodies, moving through tunnels toward light, or reliving life moments in fast-forward. A 2017 study of 159 cardiac arrest patients found 46% recalled some awareness during resuscitation, though only 9% had full NDEs with clear visions. Brain scans show oxygen starvation triggers hallucinations, but why these specific patterns? One theory: the dying brain fires random memories, and our minds stitch them into a story—like shaking a kaleidoscope until it forms a picture.

A famous case: Pam Reynolds, whose brain activity flatlined during surgery in 1991. She later described surgeons’ tools and conversations verified by the medical team. Skeptics argue anesthesia or hidden consciousness explains it, but no test can prove—or disprove—the "afterlife preview" feeling.

Three related subjects are out-of-body experiences, brain activity during clinical death, and how culture shapes afterlife beliefs.
